The name "Wanda" and the image of a husband-killer in the fate wheel, as well as the "Venus" that appear repeatedly in the her files, all come from the book "Venus in Furs" by Leopold von Sache-Masoch, which describes the Galician noble landowner Saunin falling in love with the beautiful widow Wanda. When his proposal failed, he proposed to become Wanda's slave and signed a binding agreement. In the agreement, Wanda promised Saunin to wear a fur coat as much as possible when punishing him.
As the origin of the concept of "sexual masochism" in modern psychology, the names of the male and female protagonists in the book, Saunin and Wanda, have become a symbol of the relationship between male slaves and female masters, and a model of such agreements in master-slave relationships. At the same time, the pronunciation of Saunin (Severin) is similar to the Severn River (Severn in English and Sabrina in Latin).
